---
title: "Sibling cell size asymmetry"
---

[← Back to research](../../research.qmd){.back-link}

[co-author · iScience, 2019]{.paper-meta}

::: {style="float:left; width:558px; max-width:54%; margin:0.3rem 1.6rem 0.8rem 0;"}
![](graphical-abstract.jpg){.graphical-abstract style="width:100%; max-width:100%"}

[Graphical abstract reproduced from Pham T.T., Monnard A., Helenius J., Lund E., Lee N.,
Müller D.J. &amp; Cabernard C. (2019). *Spatiotemporally Controlled Myosin Relocalization and
Internal Pressure Generate Sibling Cell Size Asymmetry.* **iScience** 13, 9–19.
[doi:10.1016/j.isci.2019.02.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.isci.2019.02.002). © 2019 The
Author(s). Reproduced under [CC BY-NC-ND 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).]{.figure-credit}
:::

An early collaboration from my developmental biology years, on which I was a co-author. Many cells
divide into two unequal-sized daughters, an asymmetry whose underlying mechanics were unclear.

Using atomic force microscopy with live-cell imaging of dividing *Drosophila* neural stem cells, we
separated the forces at work. Apical expansion is driven by hydrostatic pressure peaking just after
anaphase onset; a later rise in contractile tension at the cleavage furrow, with myosin
relocalization, drives basal expansion. When either input is disrupted, a repositioning cleavage furrow
still establishes the size asymmetry.

The result tied a long-standing developmental question, how a dividing cell sets the sizes of its
two daughters, to concrete, measurable mechanics: **pressure and cortical tension act on different
timescales**, and the furrow itself can adapt to preserve the asymmetry when one force is
perturbed. For me it was an early, formative lesson in pairing quantitative biophysics (AFM force
measurements) with live imaging to dissect a mechanism neither approach could resolve alone.

::: {style="clear:both"}
:::

To learn more: [10.1016/j.isci.2019.02.002](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.isci.2019.02.002)

[]{.section-rule}
